draft: These are Oracle/server concepts you learn for the handwritten exam — GRANT, REVOKE, and roles don't run in the browser's SQLite (it has no user accounts). Reason them out on paper the way the exam expects.

fields.label: Which SQL sublanguage do GRANT and REVOKE belong to, and what do they control?
options: DCL (Data Control Language) — they control who is permitted to run operations on database objects, as opposed to DML, which changes the data itself
DML (Data Manipulation Language) — they change the database, so they're the same family as INSERT and UPDATE
DDL (Data Definition Language) — they define structure, like CREATE and DROP
DQL (Data Query Language) — they're a form of SELECT that returns permission rows

fields.label: Why is GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON orders TO app_user better than GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON orders TO app_user for a web application's account?
options: It follows least privilege — the app gets only the two rights it needs, so a successful injection on that account still can't DELETE, DROP, or alter the table
They're equivalent; ALL is just shorter to type
GRANT ALL isn't valid SQL, so the first one is the only option that runs
GRANT ALL is slower for the database to evaluate at query time

fields.label: You run CREATE ROLE analyst; GRANT SELECT ON employee TO analyst; GRANT analyst TO jim, pam;. Later you REVOKE SELECT ON employee FROM analyst;. What happens?
options: Both Jim and Pam immediately lose SELECT on employee, because they held it through the role — revoking from the role revokes for every member at once
Nothing changes for Jim and Pam; they keep the privilege they were given
Only Jim loses it; a role-level revoke applies to just the first member listed
The analyst role is deleted, but Jim and Pam keep a private copy of the privilege

fields.label: You try GRANT SELECT ON employee TO hr_clerk in the course's browser IDE and get a syntax error. Why?
options: SQLite is a serverless, single-file database with no user accounts, so it has no GRANT/REVOKE at all — access control there is the OS file permissions; the exam's Oracle engine does support DCL
The clause order is wrong; it should be GRANT SELECT TO hr_clerk ON employee
You must CREATE USER hr_clerk first, then the GRANT works in SQLite
GRANT only works inside an explicit BEGIN ... COMMIT transaction block
